ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for paper prata or roti prata. The broader flatbread food product term (FOODON_03315571) is used as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific term for South Asian pan-fried flatbread with sweet toppings is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for roti prata or paper prata. A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for roti prata or paper prata as of 2024.

preparation_method: topped with condensed milk and sugar

serving.description: 1 piece of paper prata with condensed milk and sugar
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of paper prata with condensed milk and sugar is approximately 110 g, equivalent to one thin, crispy piece as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or Indian Muslim restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Paper Prata (Condensed Milk, Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Paper Prata (Condensed Milk, Sugar), cr_id F05020078. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical Singapore source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for pan-fried flatbread with ghee and condensed milk, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for refined wheat flatbread
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore paper prata with condensed milk and sugar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: refined wheat flatbread / chapati (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted upward for the high added sugar content from condensed milk (12.2g/100g) and the refined flour base. The sweet topping does not lower the effective GI; the combined high-GI refined flour and rapidly absorbed sugar support a GI estimate of 70 (high). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 45.3) / 100 ≈ 31.6, using 110g serving size (45.3g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (12.2g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.3g/100g) and moderate sodium (266mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sweet prata variant: the 12.2g/100g added sugar from condensed milk drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently elevates blood pressure risk (AHA 2018), compounded by 266mg/100g sodium; choose plain prata without sweet toppings and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined wheat flour base (GI ~70) combined with 12.2g/100g added sugar from condensed milk and negligible fibre (0.3g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid paper prata with condensed milk and sugar: the combination of a high-GI refined flour base (GI ~70), 12.2g/100g rapidly absorbed sugar, and only 0.3g/100g dietary fibre causes rapid and sustained postprandial blood glucose spikes; if prata is consumed, choose plain or egg variants without sweet toppings and limit to half a piece.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~4.1g/100g), high added sugar (12.2g/100g), moderate sodium (266mg/100g), and high glycaemic load per serving (~31.6)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish: the saturated fat from ghee, high added sugar from condensed milk, moderate sodium, and high glycaemic load collectively contribute to dyslipidaemia, endothelial inflammation, and elevated cardiovascular risk; occasional consumption only, with preference for plain prata variants.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and added sugar content (12.2g/100g) with negligible anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this preparation: the high refined carbohydrate load and 12.2g/100g added sugar from condensed milk promote glycaemic-driven pro-inflammatory mediators, and the absence of fibre, omega-3 fatty acids, or antioxidants provides no compensating anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paper prata with condensed milk and sugar is a popular Singaporean hawker dish of South Indian origin. It is a very thin, crispy, layered wheat-flour flatbread — the 'paper' designation refers to its near-translucent thinness compared to standard roti prata — cooked on a flat iron griddle with ghee until golden and shatteringly crisp. After cooking, it is drizzled or dipped in sweetened condensed milk and dusted with white sugar, making it one of the sweetest and most indulgent prata variants available. The dough is made from refined wheat flour, water, ghee, and salt, stretched and folded repeatedly to create thin, flaky layers before being pan-fried. Nutritionally, a 110g serving provides approximately 294 kcal, 5.4g protein, 10.1g fat, and 45.3g carbohydrate, including 13.4g sugar and only 0.3g dietary fibre. The high-GI refined flour base (GI ~70) combined with rapidly absorbed condensed milk sugar results in a high glycaemic load (~31.6 per serving), making this dish clinically significant for blood glucose management. Sodium at 266mg/100g is moderate. This dish is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar, high glycaemic load, saturated fat from ghee, and moderate sodium. It is commonly eaten as a breakfast or supper item at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and mamak restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. It is known as roti canai in Malaysia and is a cultural staple of the Singaporean Indian community.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020078), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for pan-fried flatbread with ghee and condensed milk. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for refined wheat flatbread, adjusted for high added sugar content. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.1g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous data for pan-fried refined wheat flatbread with ghee and condensed milk, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this specific item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific sweet prata preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat flatbread from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singapore paper prata with condensed milk and sugar exists in any approved GI source. 4. Serving size of 110g is a standard hawker portion estimate consistent with HPB Singapore portion guidance for prata dishes. 5. All core macros (protein_g 4.9, fat_g 9.2, carbohydrate_g 41.18) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
